title: Why Copying Others Made Humans Successful
subtitle: 模仿如何成就人类

paragraphs.en: Chimpanzees, human beings' closest animal relatives, share up to 98 percent of our genes. Yet humans and chimpanzees lead very different lives. Fewer than 300,000 wild chimpanzees live in a few forested corners of Africa today, while humans have colonized every corner of the globe. At more than 7 billion, human population dwarfs that of nearly all other mammals — despite our physical weaknesses. What could account for our species' incredible evolutionary success?
paragraphs.zh: 黑猩猩作为人类最亲近的动物近亲，与我们共享高达98%的基因。然而人类和黑猩猩的生活截然不同。如今不到30万只野生黑猩猩生活在非洲的几片森林角落，而人类已遍布全球每个角落。尽管体质孱弱，人类人口超过70亿，令几乎所有其他哺乳动物相形见绌。是什么造就了我们物种惊人的进化成功？

paragraphs.en: One obvious answer is our big brains. It could be that our raw intelligence gave us an unprecedented ability to think outside the box, innovating solutions as people migrated across the globe. But a growing number of cognitive scientists and anthropologists are rejecting that explanation. They think that we survive and thrive precisely because we don't think for ourselves. Instead, people cope with challenging climates and ecological contexts by carefully copying others.
paragraphs.zh: 一个显而易见的答案是我们的大脑。可能是我们的原始智力赋予了我们前所未有的跳出框架思考的能力，在人类迁徙全球的过程中不断创新解决方案。但越来越多的认知科学家和人类学家拒绝这一解释。他们认为我们之所以生存和繁荣，恰恰是因为我们不独立思考。相反，人们通过仔细模仿他人来应对严酷的气候和生态环境。

paragraphs.en: In a famous study, psychologists Victoria Horner and Andrew Whiten showed two groups of test subjects — children and chimpanzees — a mechanical box with a treat inside. In one condition the box was opaque, while in the other it was transparent. The experimenters demonstrated how to open the box, but they also included an irrelevant step of tapping on the box with a stick. Oddly, the children carefully copied all the steps, even when they could see the stick had no practical effect.
paragraphs.zh: 在一项著名研究中，心理学家维多利亚·霍纳和安德鲁·怀滕向两组测试对象——儿童和黑猩猩——展示了一个内藏零食的机械盒子。一种条件下盒子不透明，另一种条件下透明。实验者演示了如何打开盒子，但同时也加入了一个用棍子敲击盒子的无关步骤。奇怪的是，孩子们小心翼翼地模仿了所有步骤，即使他们看到棍子没有任何实际作用。

paragraphs.en: That is, they copied irrationally: instead of doing only what was necessary, children slavishly imitated every action they'd witnessed. Of course, that study only included three- and four-year-olds. But additional research has shown that older children and adults are even more likely to mindlessly copy others' actions. By contrast, chimpanzees in the study only over-imitated in the opaque condition. In the transparent condition — where they saw the stick was useless — they ignored that step entirely.
paragraphs.zh: 也就是说，他们进行了非理性模仿：孩子们不是只做必要之事，而是盲目模仿他们目睹的每一个动作。当然，那项研究仅涵盖三四岁儿童。但进一步研究表明，年龄较大的儿童和成人更可能无意识地模仿他人行为。相比之下，研究中的黑猩猩只在不透明条件下过度模仿。在透明条件下——看到棍子毫无用处时——它们完全跳过了那一步。

paragraphs.en: When it comes to copying, chimpanzees are more rational than human children or adults. Where does the seemingly irrational human preference for over-imitation come from? Anthropologist Joseph Henrich points out that people around the world rely on technologies that are often so complex that no one can learn them rationally. Instead, people must learn them step by step, trusting in the wisdom of more experienced elders and peers. So next time you hear someone arguing that everyone should embrace nonconformity and avoid imitating others, you might laugh a little. We're not chimpanzees, after all.
paragraphs.zh: 在模仿方面，黑猩猩比人类儿童或成人更加理性。人类看似非理性的过度模仿偏好从何而来？人类学家约瑟夫·亨里奇指出，世界各地的人们依赖的技术往往极其复杂，无人能通过理性方式学会。相反，人们必须一步步学习，信任更有经验的长辈和同伴的智慧。所以下次听到有人激情主张每个人都应拒绝从众、避免模仿他人时，你或许可以微微一笑。毕竟我们不是黑猩猩。

words.meaning: n. 不从众，不墨守成规
